1. All files and folders at the root level
2. Contents of any HTML files (just list them, note their purpose)
3. The js/ and css/ folders - list all files
4. Any package.json or config files

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have enough information. Let me compile the final report.

---

## Complete Project Analysis: c:\CLAUDE\obsidien

### 1. ROOT LEVEL FILE TREE

```
c:\CLAUDE\obsidien/
├── .claude/                    (Claude Code settings)
│   ├── settings.json           (skill overrides)
│   └── SKILL (1).md           (skill documentation)
├── css/                        (stylesheets)
│   ├── tokens.css             (design tokens, custom properties)
│   ├── base.css               (reset, grid system, typography utilities)
│   ├── header.css             (header styles)
│   ├── home.css               (page-specific styles)
│   └── footer.css             (footer styles)
├── js/                         (application logic)
│   ├── main.js                (entry point, initialization docs)
│   ├── split.js               (text character splitting)
│   ├── scroll.js              (Lenis + GSAP ScrollTrigger)
│   ├── header.js              (header color system, scroll threshold)
│   ├── menu.js                (menu/form overlays)
│   ├── places.js              (Places carousel)
│   ├── objects.js             (Objects grid)
│   ├── updates.js             (Updates carousel)
│   ├── admission.js           (Admission form handling)
│   └── webgl-stone.js         (WebGL stone relief effect)
├── index.html                 (single-page static HTML)
├── CLAUDE.md                  (project documentation)
└── BUILD_STATUS.md            (build/status file)
```

---

### 2. HTML FILES & PURPOSE

**Only one HTML file:**
- **`index.html`** — Single static multi-page clone. Contains:
  - Header with navigation (Home, Places, Objects, Request buttons, Menu)
  - Menu overlay (full-screen with navigation links)
  - Form overlay (admission request form)
  - Page transition overlay (4 columns, text)
  - Success modal
  - 9 major sections:
    - Welcome (hero with SVG arch, stone texture)
    - Places carousel (7 slides: offices with names)
    - Places-After (transition section, dark theme)
    - Objects (4 cards grid)
    - Connection (circle SVG)
    - Updates carousel (5 items)
    - People
    - Admission (form section)
    - Footer (placeholder)

---

### 3. CSS FOLDER STRUCTURE

| File | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `tokens.css` | 3424B | Design system tokens (colors, type scale, grid gap, easing) |
| `base.css` | 5737B | Reset, grid system (`.-w` class), typography utilities (`.-h0` through `.-h6`) |
| `header.css` | 6204B | Header and navigation styling |
| `home.css` | 5617B | Page sections (welcome, places, objects, etc.) |
| `footer.css` | 3788B | Footer styling |

**Key patterns:**
- All CSS custom properties defined in `tokens.css` (must load first)
- Grid system via `.-w` class: `display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(var(--g-columns, 12), 1fr)`
- Children use `--left`, `--width`, `--top`, `--height` to self-position (never hardcode `grid-column`)
- No `px` for font sizes; all use `var(--h0)` through `var(--h6)`, `var(--p)`, `var(--m)`, `var(--mm)`
- Viewport units via `--vh` and `--vw` (set by JS, not `1vh`/`1vw`)

---

### 4. JS FOLDER STRUCTURE

| File | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `main.js` | 938B | Entry point; documents initialization order |
| `split.js` | 1005B | Wraps text in `<span class="-s-char">` for animation |
| `scroll.js` | 2393B | Lenis smooth scroll, GSAP ScrollTrigger proxy, `--vh`/`--vw` calculation |
| `header.js` | 2447B | Scroll threshold (50vh), header color system (`data-header-color`) |
| `menu.js` | 3635B | Menu/form overlay open/close, `-isolate` class on `<html>` |
| `places.js` | 2168B | Places carousel (7 slides, RAF loop, prev/next buttons) |
| `objects.js` | 843B | Objects grid handling |
| `updates.js` | 1247B | Updates carousel (5 items) |
| `admission.js` | 1695B | Admission form submission |
| `webgl-stone.js` | 1504B | WebGL DynamicReliefLampEffect class (stone wall shader) |

**Initialization order (from `<script>` tags in HTML):**
1. `split.js` — Text splitting
2. `scroll.js` — Lenis + GSAP setup, sets `.-loaded` and `.-ready` on `<html>`
3. `header.js` — Scroll listener
4. `menu.js` — Overlay handlers
5. Carousel modules (`places.js`, `objects.js`, `updates.js`, `admission.js`)
6. `main.js` — Final entry point

---

### 5. PACKAGE.JSON & CONFIG FILES

**None present.** This is a **plain HTML/CSS/JS project with no build step**, no package manager, no bundler.

---

### 6. LIBRARIES & FRAMEWORKS (CDN ONLY)

Loaded via `<script>` and `<link>` tags in `index.html`:

**CSS/Animation Libraries:**
- **Lenis** (smooth scroll) — `https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@studio-freight/lenis/bundled/lenis.min.css`
- **Google Fonts** — Cormorant Garamond, DM Serif Display, Inter

**JavaScript Libraries:**
- **GSAP 3.12.5** (animation engine) — `https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/gsap/3.12.5/gsap.min.js`
- **GSAP ScrollTrigger 3.12.5** (scroll animations) — `https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/gsap/3.12.5/ScrollTrigger.min.js`
- **Lenis** (smooth scroll engine) — `https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@studio-freight/lenis/bundled/lenis.min.js`

**No React, Vue, Svelte, Webpack, Vite, or other frameworks.**

---

### 7. OVERALL ARCHITECTURE

**Build System:** **None** — Pure static site. Served via `npx serve .` or any static HTTP server.

**Global State Management:** **DOM classes on `<html>`**
| Class | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `.-loaded` | Lenis/GSAP initialized, CSS transitions enabled |
| `.-ready` | Content visible, header animation in |
| `.-scrolled` | ScrollY > 50vh, header compact mode |
| `.-dark` | Current section has dark header theme |
| `.-isolate` | Menu/form open, scroll locked |

**Animation System:**
- **Scroll-driven:** GSAP ScrollTrigger with `scrub: true`, writing CSS custom properties (e.g., `--progress`, `--slide-progress`)
- **Text split:** Characters wrapped in `<span class="-s-char">` with `--char-index` for stagger on hover
- **Carousels:** RAF loop (6000ms interval), progress → CSS custom property → CSS `calc()` transforms
- **WebGL:** `DynamicReliefLampEffect` class for stone relief effect (see `webgl-stone.js`)

**Page Linking:**
- Single `index.html` file
- Navigation links (`/`, `/places`, `/objects`, `/about`, `/people`) are placeholders (static clone references only)
- Page transitions via `.page-transition` overlay (4 column spans with `--order` custom properties)
- Transition fires on `<a>` click (before `window.location`)

**Key Architectural Decisions:**
1. **No JavaScript modules/imports** — all files are globals, loaded sequentially
2. **CSS-driven animations** — JS writes custom properties, CSS does transforms/opacity via `calc()`
3. **Data attributes for behavior** — `data-split`, `data-header-color`, `data-action` drive functionality
4. **Mobile-aware** — viewport units calculated in JS to account for browser chrome (`--vh`, `--vw`)
5. **Handmade DOM** — no virtual DOM, no state framework; imperative JS manipulates classes/styles directly

---

## Summary

This is a **pixel-faithful, hand-crafted static HTML/CSS/JS clone** of obsidianassembly.com with a dark stone aesthetic. It uses **GSAP + Lenis for animations and smooth scroll**, **pure CSS Grid with custom properties** for layout, and **DOM classes for global state**. No build step, no package manager, no framework—just three CDN libraries (Lenis, GSAP, ScrollTrigger) and hand-authored code. Designed to be served directly from disk or via a simple static server.

| `webgl-stone.js` | 1504B | WebGL DynamicReliefLampEffect class (stone wall shader) |

**Initialization order (from `<script>` tags in HTML):**
1. `split.js` — Text splitting
2. `scroll.js` — Lenis + GSAP setup, sets `.-loaded` and `.-ready` on `<html>`
3. `header.js` — Scroll listener
4. `menu.js` — Overlay handlers
5. Carousel modules (`places.js`, `objects.js`, `updates.js`, `admission.js`)
6. `main.js` — Final entry point

---

### 5. PACKAGE.JSON & CONFIG FILES

**None present.** This is a **plain HTML/CSS/JS project with no build step**, no package manager, no bundler.

---

### 6. LIBRARIES & FRAMEWORKS (CDN ONLY)

Loaded via `<script>` and `<link>` tags in `index.html`:

**CSS/Animation Libraries:**
- **Lenis** (smooth scroll) — `https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@studio-freight/lenis/bundled/lenis.min.css`
- **Google Fonts** — Cormorant Garamond, DM Serif Display, Inter

**JavaScript Libraries:**
- **GSAP 3.12.5** (animation engine) — `https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/gsap/3.12.5/gsap.min.js`
- **GSAP ScrollTrigger 3.12.5** (scroll animations) — `https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/gsap/3.12.5/ScrollTrigger.min.js`
- **Lenis** (smooth scroll engine) — `https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@studio-freight/lenis/bundled/lenis.min.js`

**No React, Vue, Svelte, Webpack, Vite, or other frameworks.**

---

### 7. OVERALL ARCHITECTURE

**Build System:** **None** — Pure static site. Served via `npx serve .` or any static HTTP server.

**Global State Management:** **DOM classes on `<html>`**
| Class | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `.-loaded` | Lenis/GSAP initialized, CSS transitions enabled |
| `.-ready` | Content visible, header animation in |
| `.-scrolled` | ScrollY > 50vh, header compact mode |
| `.-dark` | Current section has dark header theme |
| `.-isolate` | Menu/form open, scroll locked |

**Animation System:**
- **Scroll-driven:** GSAP ScrollTrigger with `scrub: true`, writing CSS custom properties (e.g., `--progress`, `--slide-progress`)
- **Text split:** Characters wrapped in `<span class="-s-char">` with `--char-index` for stagger on hover
- **Carousels:** RAF loop (6000ms interval), progress → CSS custom property → CSS `calc()` transforms
- **WebGL:** `DynamicReliefLampEffect` class for stone relief effect (see `webgl-stone.js`)

**Page Linking:**
- Single `index.html` file
- Navigation links (`/`, `/places`, `/objects`, `/about`, `/people`) are placeholders (static clone references only)
- Page transitions via `.page-transition` overlay (4 column spans with `--order` custom properties)
- Transition fires on `<a>` click (before `window.location`)

**Key Architectural Decisions:**
1. **No JavaScript modules/imports** — all files are globals, loaded sequentially
2. **CSS-driven animations** — JS writes custom properties, CSS does transforms/opacity via `calc()`
3. **Data attributes for behavior** — `data-split`, `data-header-color`, `data-action` drive functionality
4. **Mobile-aware** — viewport units calculated in JS to account for browser chrome (`--vh`, `--vw`)
5. **Handmade DOM** — no virtual DOM, no state framework; imperative JS manipulates classes/styles directly

---

## Summary

This is a **pixel-faithful, hand-crafted static HTML/CSS/JS clone** of obsidianassembly.com with a dark stone aesthetic. It uses **GSAP + Lenis for animations and smooth scroll**, **pure CSS Grid with custom properties** for layout, and **DOM classes for global state**. No build step, no package manager, no framework—just three CDN libraries (Lenis, GSAP, ScrollTrigger) and hand-authored code. Designed to be served directly from disk or via a simple static server.

## How This Project Works

**No Vite. No build step. No framework.** This is a hand-crafted static site.

---

### Build System
Nothing. Literally zero tooling. You serve it with:
```bash
npx serve .
# or
python -m http.server 8080
```

No `package.json`, no `node_modules`, no bundler, no compiler.

---

### Tech Stack

| Layer | What's Used |
|---|---|
| Structure | Single `index.html` (all sections on one page) |
| Styles | 5 hand-written CSS files (`tokens.css`, `base.css`, `header.css`, `home.css`, `footer.css`) |
| Scripts | 10 hand-written JS files loaded via `<script>` tags (no modules, no imports) |
| Animation | **GSAP 3.12.5** + **ScrollTrigger** via CDN |
| Smooth scroll | **Lenis** via CDN |
| WebGL | Vanilla WebGL (custom shader in `js/webgl-stone.js`) |
| Fonts | Google Fonts CDN |

---

### Architecture Philosophy

**JS writes CSS variables, CSS does the actual rendering.**

Every scroll-driven animation works like this:
1. GSAP ScrollTrigger updates `--progress` (a CSS custom property)
2. CSS `calc()` consumes it for transforms, opacity, position
3. JS never touches `element.style.transform` directly

**Global state = classes on `<html>`:**
- `.-loaded` → Lenis/GSAP initialized
- `.-ready` → page visible
- `.-scrolled` → past 50vh
- `.-dark` → current section is dark-themed
- `.-isolate` → menu open, scroll locked

**Layout = CSS Grid with custom properties:**
```css
.-w { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(12, 1fr); }
.child { --left: 3; --width: 4; } /* grid-column: 3 / span 4 */
```

---

### Why Not Vite?

This is intentionally a **pixel-faithful clone** of [obsidianassembly.com](https://obsidianassembly.com), which itself appears to use a proprietary engine (`$stringTune`). The clone replaces that with GSAP/Lenis but keeps the same no-build, hand-authored approach for simplicity and fidelity. It's a design reference/clone, not a production app.

content.thinking:
Now I have a full picture of the project. Let me plan what needs to be done:

**Priority order from BUILD_STATUS.md:**
1. Add placeholder images — Unblocks all visuals
2. Complete footer layout — Full content + nav grid
3. Welcome SVG arch animation — GSAP ScrollTrigger scrub
4. Connection section parallax — GSAP ScrollTrigger
5. WebGL stone effect (or CSS fallback)
6. Inner page stubs — places.html, objects.html, about.html, people.html, 404.html
7. Page transition animation — On link clicks

For images, since we can't download from the internet, I'll create SVG placeholders that look good. 

I need to generate several key assets: a dark stone texture, architectural interior photos, a silhouette figure, and various SVG masks and paths for the animations and layout effects.

For the SVG files, I can create those directly. For the photographic images, I'm considering whether to use actual image downloads, gradient-based SVG placeholders, or canvas-generated WebP files—but I need to be careful about generating URLs without confidence they'll work. I think SVG placeholders that capture the dark aesthetic would be the most reliable approach here.

The real constraint is that the CSS has hardcoded references to specific image paths and formats like `images/stone-wall.webp` and `images/mask-fade-20.webp`, so I'd need to either create files matching those exact names or update the CSS references themselves.

I think the best solution is to create SVG placeholder files but save them with the expected extensions—browsers will render SVG content regardless of the file extension. For the stone wall and office images, I'll use dark gradient fills to create a cohesive look that fits the design.

Creating SVG placeholders... I'm realizing I need to be more careful about which image formats actually work with CSS mask properties—some need to be raster images rather than SVG, so I'll need to create the appropriate file types for each placeholder and update the CSS references accordingly. Downloading placeholder images...

I'm settling on using picsum.photos with deterministic seeds to get consistent dark images for the office placeholders, then creating the SVG mask files and completing the rest of the page structure—the arch animation, parallax effects, inner page stubs, and transition logic.

But I'm noticing a potential issue with the script setup: the HTML is loading regular scripts without the `type="module"` attribute, yet the JavaScript files are using `export` statements, which should cause a SyntaxError in non-module scripts. I need to verify whether this is actually breaking the code or if something else is handling it.

Looking at the exports across the files—lenis and updateViewportUnits from scroll.js, the menu functions from menu.js, and the DynamicReliefLampEffect from webgl-stone.js—none of these appear to be imported anywhere else in the codebase. The simplest fix would be to just remove the export statements entirely, which aligns with the no-build-step architecture mentioned in the documentation, rather than converting everything to ES modules.

Now I'm mapping out the full plan: first I'll strip those unnecessary exports, then move into the image assets phase where I need to create the SVG files for the star mask, footer path, footer mask, and rounded rect, plus handle the webp fade masks. After that I'll need to grab placeholder images for the office and team sections.

For the fade mask images, they're being used as CSS mask-image properties, so I need to figure out if I should create them as SVGs or find another approach that works with the mask-image property. I'm settling on a practical approach: use SVG files for the masks with proper `.svg` extensions in the CSS, download office photos from picsum.photos with different seeds, and create or source a dark stone texture for the wall background. Now I'll implement this systematically by setting up the directory structure, downloading the images via PowerShell, generating the SVG assets, fixing the JavaScript export issues, and completing the footer HTML.

After that, I need to add the Welcome arch animation, wire up the Connection parallax effect, implement the transition system, and create stub pages for the inner sections. For the mask gradients specifically, I'll use inline SVG data URIs in the CSS to avoid file format complications entirely. Writing SVG star mask...

Now I need to figure out which SVG files are actually referenced in the footer CSS and what they're supposed to do.

Looking at the footer styles, I can see that `star-mask.svg` is definitely being used, while `footer-path.svg`, `footer-mask.svg`, and `r16.svg` appear to be listed in the asset inventory but aren't currently referenced anywhere in the CSS I've reviewed. They might be intended for future use or were part of an earlier design iteration.

For the immediate priority, I should focus on creating the assets that are actively being used: the star mask SVG, the stone wall texture, and the fade mask—though I can replace that last one with a CSS gradient instead of generating an image file. After that, I'll need to generate the office photos.

Now I'm thinking through how to handle the stone wall texture. I could download a ready-made dark stone image, but creating one with SVG turbulence filters would be more efficient and give me better control. Though I'm realizing there's a potential issue with the file naming—if I save an SVG as `stone-wall.webp`, the server will serve it with the wrong MIME type based on the extension alone, which would break the rendering.

So I need to stick with actual raster images for these files. I'll use PowerShell to batch download textures from picsum.photos—a dark stone texture for the footer, seven different dark images for the office section, and a silhouette-style image for the guys section. The mask-fade images I can replace with CSS gradients instead.

Before I start downloading, I should double-check the `scroll.js` file since it's using `export` statements in what looks like a non-module script. That would normally be a syntax error, so I need to verify whether this is actually causing problems or if there's something else going on.

Actually, looking at the script tags in the HTML, `webgl-stone.js` isn't even being loaded, so that's not the issue. But `scroll.js` and `menu.js` are definitely included, and if they're throwing syntax errors on the `export` statements, that would break the entire script chain.

The real question is whether the browser parses the entire file before executing it, or if it runs code as it goes—because if `export` is at the bottom of the file after all the actual logic, maybe the functions are already defined before the error gets hit.

Actually, JavaScript always parses the complete file before any execution happens, so a syntax error anywhere means nothing in that file runs at all. That means `scroll.js` and `menu.js` are completely broken, which would disable smooth scrolling, viewport initialization, and the menu—but the BUILD_STATUS.md claims these features are working. Either the build status is outdated or I'm misunderstanding how the syntax error behaves in practice. I should verify this by actually testing it in the browser.

Let me reconsider whether `export` is truly a syntax error in classic scripts. It's a reserved word, so yes, using it outside of module context should throw a SyntaxError. The fix is straightforward: remove those `export` statements from the non-module scripts. Now I'll map out the full action plan: fix the critical JavaScript bugs by stripping the exports, set up the image directory structure, and start downloading the necessary assets.

The user is saying the website looks bad visually. I need to look at the actual site to understand what's wrong. Let me first check the current state of the code to understand what might be causing visual issues, then I'll try to serve the site and take a screenshot, or at least do a thorough code review.

Let me look at the key CSS files carefully to understand the layout issues:

1. The grid system - children need `--left` and `--width` set, but looking at home.css, many elements have `data-header-color` as a CSS property which is not valid CSS
2. The `.welcome-section .-w` has `--width: 8; --left: 3;` - but `-w` is the grid container itself, not a child of a grid. 

I'm seeing the grid setup now - the `-w` class creates a 12-column grid layout, and child elements use CSS custom properties to position themselves within it, with the grid-column calculations based on the `--left` and `--width` variables. I see the issue now—the `.-w` container itself isn't a grid, so setting custom properties on it doesn't position it. Instead, those variables cascade down to the children of `.-w`, which are the actual grid items. So when the CSS sets `--width: 8` and `--left: 3` on `.-w`, all its children inherit those values and end up positioned at the same grid columns, which means they'd all stack on top of each other unless individual children override those variables. I see the issue now—all child elements inherit `--left: 3` and `--width: 8`, placing them in columns 3-11, but they'd also all be in row 1 with the default `--top: 1` and `--height: 1`, causing them to overlap completely rather than stack vertically. Let me check the actual CSS rules to understand how the grid positioning is supposed to work. I see the grid system expects every element to have its own `--top` property for vertical stacking, but the HTML doesn't set these values, so everything collapses into the first row and overlaps. The visual result would be text layered over images, elements squeezed into a single grid cell, and the header looking completely broken. I'm also noticing there's invalid CSS like using `data-header-color` as a property instead of reading it as an attribute. I see the issue now—the `.-w > *` rule explicitly sets `--left: 1` and `--width: 1` on each child, which overrides any inherited values from the parent container. content.text:
Here's exactly what was wrong and what was fixed:

---

**Root cause #1 — everything was 1/12th wide and stacked (the main alien)**

`base.css` had this in `.-w > *`:
```css
--width: 1;   /* ← set on every child, overriding inherited values */
--left: 1;
grid-column-end: calc(var(--left, 1) + var(--width, 1));  /* col 1–2 only */
```

Every single element on the page — headings, images, forms — was squished into a single 1-of-12 column, roughly 8% of the page width. Any parent-level constraint like `.welcome-section .-w { --width: 8; }` was silently ignored because the child rule always won.

Fixed to:
```css
grid-column: var(--left, 1) / span var(--width, 12);  /* inherits parent values, defaults to full width */
```

---

**Root cause #2 — everything in every section was overlapping at row 1**

`grid-row: 1 / 2` on every child meant the h1, the SVG, the stone image, and the carousel all sat on top of each other in the same grid row. Now: sections use auto-placement (elements stack vertically). Only the header and overlays force `grid-row: 1` — which is correct there, since nav items need to share a horizontal row.

---

**Root cause #3 — invalid CSS in home.css**

`data-header-color: light;` appeared inside CSS rules for every section — completely invalid (browsers silently ignore unknown properties, but it polluted the file).

---

**Other fixes:**
- Places section changed to `display: block` so the sticky canvas layer and carousel stack correctly (flex column fought with sticky)
- Objects section same treatment for the sticky card grid
- Overlay `.close`/`.underlay` decorative divs are now `position: absolute` so they don't push grid items around

Run `npx serve .` and open `http://localhost:3000` — should look like a site now.
